title: Electrodeposition of Thin Films and Multilayers on Silicon
abstract:
phys. stat. sol. (a) 173, 73 (1999)
Subject classification: 68.55.Jk; 75.70.Pa; S1.2; S5.11
Electrodeposition of Thin Films and Multilayers on Silicon
A. A. Pasa1) (a) and W. Schwarzacher (b)
(a) Departamento de FõÂsica, UFSC, 88040-900, FlorianoÂpolis-SC, Brazil
(b) H. H. Wills Physics Laboratory, Tyndall Avenue, Bristol BS8 1TL, U.K.
(Received September 20, 1998)
Cu, Co and Ni thin films and Co±Ni±Cu/Cu multilayers have been electrodeposited directly on
n-type silicon substrates. This removes the need of using a seed-layer deposited by some other meth-
ods as a part of the growth process and integrates an efficient, inexpensive and convenient method
for fabricating thin films with silicon technology. The deposits were prepared under potentiostatic
conditions from different aqueous solutions, containing basically: (i) sulphates of the metallic ions
plus sodium sulphate and boric acid for thin films and (ii) Ni sulphamate, Co sulphate, Cu sul-
phate, boric acid and sulphamic acid for multilayers. Aspects related to the deposition process and
deposited layers were investigated by cyclic voltammetry, current transients, scanning electron mi-
croscopy, Rutherford backscattering and magnetoresistance measurements. Typically, thin compact
metallic layers with regular granularity were obtained. Depending on the additives used, different
nucleation and growth mechanisms were observed. Magnetic multilayers with a maximum giant
magnetoresistive (GMR) ratio of about 10% were produced.
1. Introduction
The electrodeposition technique has a major advantage over other methods of thin film
production, namely, the possibility of performing deposition at normal conditions of
pressure and temperature, requiring relatively inexpensive equipment. Recently, electro-
deposition gained renewed attention with the adoption by major companies of a copper
plating process for the fabrication of interconnects in integrated circuits [1, 2]. In this
case, the electroplating technique is especially interesting due to its low cost, high
throughput, high quality of the deposits and excellent via/trench filling capabilities. Sev-
eral groups have also reported current-in-plane giant magnetoresistance (GMR) for
electrodeposited multilayers consisting of alternating layers of a magnetic and a non-
magnetic metal deposited from a single electrolyte [3].
Nevertheless, electrodeposition has one feature that can be a major disadvantage,
namely the need to use a conducting substrate. This disadvantage can be circumvented
by employing evaporated or sputtered seed layers, i.e., very thin metallic layers used to
electrodeposit metals onto insulating or semiconducting substrates [4]. However, semicon-
ducting substrates can conduct sufficiently well to allow electrodeposition, making the
direct electrodeposition of magnetic thin films and multilayers directly on semiconduc-
tors a subject of fundamental and practical significance. Such deposition could lead to
the integration of an efficient, inexpensive and convenient method for fabricating thin
layers with silicon technology, for example. In recent work, magnetic multilayers di-
1 Corresponding author. e-mail: pasa@fisica.ufsc.br; Fax:++55(0)48 331 9946
A. A. Pasa and W. Schwarzacher: Electrodeposition of Thin Films and Multilayers on Silicon
73
 rectly electrodeposited on silicon showed a giant magnetoresistive (GMR) effect of
about 10% with a sensitivity of over 0.04%/Oe [5] and spin-valves directly electrode-
posited on GaAs a magnetoresistive change of up to 6% with high sensitivities ran-
ging up to 0.67% Oe [6].
Surprisingly, the direct electrodeposition on silicon has been the subject of relatively
few reports [7 to 14]. In this paper, we will study the electrodeposition of thin films and
multilayers of the metals Cu, Co and Ni on the surface of n-type (100)Si substrates.
Different aqueous electrolytes containing sulphate or sulphamate of the metals and sup-
porting electrolytes/additives were used. The electrochemical aspects of the deposition
were analysed by cyclic voltammetry and current transients and deposits by scanning elec-
tron microscopy (SEM), Rutherford backscattering and magnetoresistive measurements.
2. Experimental
The substrates used in our experiments were single side polished, technical grade (100)
oriented Si wafers n doped for a resistivity of 3 to 7 Wcm, and electrical contact to each
substrate was made through a GaIn or GaAl back contact. An adhesive tape was used
to mask off all the substrate except for the area on which deposition was desired. Each
substrate was cleaned with 10% HF solution, then immediately transferred to the elec-
trodeposition cell. In order to minimize chemical reactions between the substrate and
the constituents of the electrolyte, the time between immersion and application of po-
tential control was kept to a minimum, and generally did not exceed 7 s. All the elec-
trolytes, as well as the etching solutions used to clean the samples prior to the electro-
chemical experiments, were prepared from filtered deionized water with a resistivity of
18 MW cm and analytical grade reagents. A three-electrode cell was used, together with
a computer-controlled potentiostat. The potentials were measured against a saturated
calomel electrode (SCE) which was placed as close as possible to the Si surface to
minimize the ohmic potential drop in the electrolyte. The Pt foil counter electrode was
placed directly opposite the working electrode (substrate).
Specifically for the electrodeposition of the multilayers, the applied potential was
switched between the deposition potential for Cu and for the ferromagnetic Co±Ni±Cu
alloy whenever the charge passed reached the value corresponding to the desired layer
thickness. For all multilayer samples, the first layer to be deposited was a 100 A layer
of Co±Ni±Cu, which was chosen to give good coverage and adhesion to the Si sub-
strate. All quoted layer thicknesses are nominal thicknesses, calculated from the current
passed during deposition assuming bulk densities and 100% current efficiency, though
in practice the current efficiency for Co±Ni±Cu alloy deposition will be significantly
lower due to hydrogen evolution. All magnetoresistive (MR) measurements were car-
ried out using four point-probes arranged in a square, making pressure contact to the
sample. The longitudinal MR is the ratio of the potential difference measured between
the contacts on a side of the square parallel to the applied field and the current be-
tween the contacts on the opposite side [3].
3. Results and Discussion
We will present the results obtained by electrodepositing thin films of Cu, Co and Ni
and multilayers of these elements. Emphasis will be given to different aspects in each

Surprisingly, the direct electrodeposition on silicon has been the subject of relatively
few reports [7 to 14]. In this paper, we will study the electrodeposition of thin films and
multilayers of the metals Cu, Co and Ni on the surface of n-type (100)Si substrates.
Different aqueous electrolytes containing sulphate or sulphamate of the metals and sup-
porting electrolytes/additives were used. The electrochemical aspects of the deposition
were analysed by cyclic voltammetry and current transients and deposits by scanning elec-
tron microscopy (SEM), Rutherford backscattering and magnetoresistive measurements.
The substrates used in our experiments were single side polished, technical grade (100)
oriented Si wafers n doped for a resistivity of 3 to 7 Wcm, and electrical contact to each
substrate was made through a GaIn or GaAl back contact. An adhesive tape was used
to mask off all the substrate except for the area on which deposition was desired. Each
substrate was cleaned with 10% HF solution, then immediately transferred to the elec-
trodeposition cell. In order to minimize chemical reactions between the substrate and
the constituents of the electrolyte, the time between immersion and application of po-
tential control was kept to a minimum, and generally did not exceed 7 s. All the elec-
trolytes, as well as the etching solutions used to clean the samples prior to the electro-
chemical experiments, were prepared from filtered deionized water with a resistivity of
18 MW cm and analytical grade reagents. A three-electrode cell was used, together with
a computer-controlled potentiostat. The potentials were measured against a saturated
calomel electrode (SCE) which was placed as close as possible to the Si surface to
minimize the ohmic potential drop in the electrolyte. The Pt foil counter electrode was
placed directly opposite the working electrode (substrate).
Specifically for the electrodeposition of the multilayers, the applied potential was
switched between the deposition potential for Cu and for the ferromagnetic Co±Ni±Cu
alloy whenever the charge passed reached the value corresponding to the desired layer
thickness. For all multilayer samples, the first layer to be deposited was a 100 A layer
of Co±Ni±Cu, which was chosen to give good coverage and adhesion to the Si sub-
strate. All quoted layer thicknesses are nominal thicknesses, calculated from the current
passed during deposition assuming bulk densities and 100% current efficiency, though
in practice the current efficiency for Co±Ni±Cu alloy deposition will be significantly
lower due to hydrogen evolution. All magnetoresistive (MR) measurements were car-
ried out using four point-probes arranged in a square, making pressure contact to the
sample. The longitudinal MR is the ratio of the potential difference measured between
the contacts on a side of the square parallel to the applied field and the current be-
tween the contacts on the opposite side [3].
We will present the results obtained by electrodepositing thin films of Cu, Co and Ni
and multilayers of these elements. Emphasis will be given to different aspects in each
A. A. Pasa and W. Schwarzacher
case, for example, the nucleation and growth processes for copper thin layers, hydrogen
evolution during cobalt deposition, the electrodeposition rate of nickel layers and the
magnetoresistive properties of multilayers.
For the electrodeposition of Cu thin films two different electrolytes were used: electro-
lyte A containing 0.013 M CuSO4 and 0.50 M Na2SO4 and electrolyte B with 0.013 M
CuSO4, 0.186 M HF and 0.5 M H3BO3. A typical cyclic voltammogram using a Si elec-
trode is shown in Fig. 1. A large nucleation loop, associated with electrodeposition on a
foreign substrate, and a cathodic peak resulting from the reduction of Cu2+ were ob-
served. This type of current versus voltage curve facilitates the choice of the potentials
to be applied for the deposition experiments and resulting current transients.
Figs. 2a and b show the current transients obtained with the two different electrolytes
and for four different potential steps selected near the onset of the cathodic current in
the voltammogram. The curves have the expected behaviour with the characteristic
peak corresponding to the nucleation-and-growth process and, for longer times, merg-
ing into a common curve which is caused by diffusion control and described by the
Cottrell equation [15]. In Figs. 2c and d we present the transients as reduced-variable
plots to enable comparison with a standard model for three-dimensional nucleation
followed by diffusion-limited growth [16, 17]. These plots are obtained from Figs. 2a
and b by normalizing the two variables i and t with respect to the current maximum
imax and the time tmax at which the current has its maximum. In Figs. 2c and d the
theoretical reduced plots for instantaneous and progressive nucleation are also shown
Electrodeposition of Thin Films and Multilayers on Silicon
Fig. 1. Cyclic voltammograms (20 mV/s) obtained with electrolyte containing 0.013 M CuSO4 and
[17]. From a comparison of the experimental and theoretical curves two different nu-
cleation mechanisms for the electrodeposition of copper on n-type Si are apparent,
namely an instantaneous nucleation process for solutions containing sodium sulphate
and a progressive one for solutions containing boric acid and hydrofluoric acid, as seen
in Figs. 2c and d, respectively. In both cases, the theoretical model adequately describes
the experimental data, although for electrolyte B and applied potentials of ±±0.44 and
ÿ0.47 V, a deviation from the model in the region controlled by diffusion was ob-
To confirm the results predicted by the nucleation-and-growth model, the deposits
were studied by SEM. Fig. 3a shows the superficial morphology of a sample deposited
A. A. Pasa and W. Schwarzacher
Fig. 2. Current transients obtained with a) electrolyte A and b) electrolyte B, for four different poten-
tial steps and reduced-variable plots c) and d) of the transients shown in a) and b), respectively. Theo-
retical curves for instantaneous and progressive nucleation are also shown for comparison
from the solution containing Na2SO4, for 5 s at ±±0.44 V. As can be seen, a high areal
density of grains was formed, where all grains had practically the same size and were
distributed homogeneously on the surface of the Si substrate. A completely different
behaviour was observed with the solution containing H3BO3 and HF, where a low den-
sity distribution of different-sized grains was formed on the Si surface (see Fig. 3b).
In Figs. 3c and d the final morphology of the deposits after 300 s of growth are
shown. These micrographs suggest a more regular and less rough surface in samples
prepared by instantaneous nucleation processes [14].
Cobalt thin films were obtained from a solution containing 0.104 M cobalt sulphate,
0.5 M sodium sulphate and 0.5 M boric acid. Fig. 4 shows an SEM image of the super-
Electrodeposition of Thin Films and Multilayers on Silicon
Fig. 3. SEM micrographs of deposits obtained with solutions containing 0.013 M CuSO4 + 0.5 M
Na2SO4 at a) ±±0.44 V (5 s) and c) ±±0.42 V (300 s) and 0.013 M CuSO4 + 0.186 M HF + 0.5 M
H3BO3 at b) ±±0.44V (5 s) and d) ±±0.40 V (300 s)
ficial morphology of a sample prepared at ±±1.08 V for 100 s. A compact and granular
In order to characterize structurally and compositionally the electrodeposited layers,
RBS measurements were performed. Fig. 5 shows a representative RBS spectrum ob-
tained from a deposit prepared at ±±1.11 V for 100 s. The RBS analysis showed that
uniform cobalt layers with thicknesses varying between 2000 and 2500A were formed
for a deposition time of 100 s at different applied potentials.
As expected for the cobalt system, the application of very negative potentials pro-
motes the evolution of hydrogen simultaneously with the reduction of the metal. The
presence of hydrogen evolution is revealed, for example, by an exponential growth of
A. A. Pasa and W. Schwarzacher
Fig. 4. SEM micrograph showing the edge of a cobalt thin film electrodeposited at ±±1.08 V for
100 s. The electrolyte contained 0.104 M CoSO4, 0.5 M Na2SO4 and 0.5 M H3BO3
Fig. 5. RBS spectrum of Co film
electrodeposited on Si at ±±1.11 V
for 100 s from an electrolyte con-
the cathodic current in the voltammetric curves and, also, by the formation of hydrogen
bubbles. The presence of holes (dark regions) on the surface of the metal layers, as
seen by SEM measurements, is associated with the formation of hydrogen bubbles. For
samples where holes were observed, the RBS spectra revealed the existence of uncov-
ered regions on the silicon surface [18].
To investigate the influence of hydrogen evolution, the RBS technique is an appro-
priate tool, because it allows us to calculate the number of atoms per unit area and,
consequently, the amount of charge per square centimetre Qcobalt needed to reduce the
electrodeposited cobalt ions. Dividing Qcobalt by the deposition time we obtain the
mean cobalt current density icobalt. A similar procedure was adopted by Allongue and
Souteyrand [19]. By comparison with the mean total deposition current itotal we deter-
mined the efficiency of the process. The mean itotal was obtained by integrating the
current transients and dividing them by the corresponding deposition time. For the
cobalt electrolyte used, the current transients are very similar to those displayed in
Fig. 6 shows the dependence of itotal and icobalt on the applied potential. As observed,
for less negative applied potentials the influence of hydrogen evolution is very low and
the efficiency of the process is higher than 93%. More negative values favour hydrogen
evolution and the current increases rapidly. Fig. 7 shows the dependence of the mean
current densities itotal and icobalt on the cobalt sulphate concentration. In spite of the
scatter in the icobalt data, both curves show a parallel behaviour, indicating that the
hydrogen partial current remains constant with the increase of the cobalt sulphate con-
centration. These results let us conclude that the H2 evolution and the reduction of the
cobalt are independent processes, as assumed for the electrodeposition of Pt on GaAs
[19], and that less negative potentials hinder H2 evolution.

High quality Ni thin films were obtained with an aqueous electrolyte containing 1.0 M
nickel sulphate, 1.0 M sodium sulphate and 0.5 M boric acid [20]. Fig. 8 illustrates typi-
cal Ni layers obtained on silicon, at ±±1.0 V for 300 s, where a metallic appearance and
granular and compact morphology were observed.
Further characterizations of the electrodeposited nickel layers were done with RBS.
Fig. 9 shows a sequence of RBS spectra obtained as a function of the deposition time
at ±±1.0 V. Uniform layers with increasing thicknesses are clearly seen. Additionally, the
thickness of the films calculated from the width of the RBS depth profiles grows line-

arly with time, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 9, with a growth rate of about 45 A/s.
For this system efficiencies higher than 75% were obtained.
Fig. 10 displays a typical current versus voltage curve for an electrolyte containing low
concentrations of two different metal sulphates, i.e., Cu and Ni. The peak at ±±0.4 V corre-

Co±Ni±Cu/Cu magnetic multilayers were prepared from a solution containing
2.3 M nickel sulphamate, 0.41 M cobalt sulphate, 0.05 M copper sulphate, 0.49 M
boric acid and 0.04 M sulphamic acid. A similar electrolyte was successfully used to

Fig. 13 shows the longitudinal MR for a film consisting of 20 repeats of 20 A Co±
Ni±Cu/10 A Cu electrodeposited on Si with a 100 A Co±Ni±Cu layer. The giant
magnetoresistance does not saturate even in an applied field of 8 kOe, which suggests
that it is associated with strong antiferromagnetic coupling between the magnetic
For these electrodeposited multilayers a strong dependence of the MR ratio on the
thickness of the nonmagnetic layer tCu and on the thickness of the ferromagnetic one
tCo±Ni±Cu was observed [5]. Saturation occurs for large values of tCu at relatively low
applied fields (see Fig. 12), which is consistent with the GMR being associated with
uncoupled magnetic layers. For small values of tCu saturation does not occur (see fig-
ure 13), which could be associated with an antiferromagnetic coupling of the magnetic
layers. The electrodeposited film appears to be made up of grains (see Fig. 11), the
boundaries of which could be the site for much of the ferromagnetic coupling that is
postulated to lower the GMR for small values of tCu [3].
Electrodeposition of Thin Films and Multilayers on Silicon

Electrodeposition is an appropriate and inexpensive method for the production of thin
films and multilayers on Si. High quality layers with metallic appearance and granular
morphology were obtained. For the electrodeposition of Cu thin films, instantaneous
and progressive nucleation mechanisms were observed depending on the electrolyte
used. Co thin films with a minimum contribution of hydrogen evolution and Ni thin
films with a constant growth rate of about 45 A/s were prepared. Co±Ni±Cu/Cu multi-
layers with a MR ratio of about 10% and a sensitivity of over 0.04%/Oe were pro-
duced, which may have potential for sensor applications.
The authors would like to acknowledge A. P. O'Keeffe, O. I. Ka-
syutich, S. G. dos Santos Filho and the staff of LaboratoÂrio de Filmes Finos e Super-
fõÂcies for helping with the whole development of this work. They also would like to
acknowledge the support of the DFG and DAAD (Germany), British Council (U.K.)
and CNPq, CNPq/PADCT III and CAPES (Brazil).
